# S3 integration (correct + secure)

This project uses S3 as the **evidence + media store**: raw evidence bundles, screenshots, generated media, audio, and rendered assets (see the slim stack in the project spec). S3 is written from two very different trust zones — the **GitHub Actions LangGraph worker** (Python/TS, server-side, full credentials) and the **Vercel/v0 Next.js dashboard** (browser-facing, must never hold long-lived keys). Get the trust boundary right first; everything else follows.

> Core rule: the browser/dashboard tier never gets AWS credentials. It gets **presigned URLs** minted server-side (Next.js API route or the worker), scoped to one object + one method + a short TTL.

## Pull current docs first

S3 security and defaults change (e.g. SSE-C was disabled by default for new buckets in April 2026). Re-fetch before relying on specifics:

- S3 security best practices — https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/security-best-practices.html
- Presigned URLs (download/upload) — https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/using-presigned-url.html
- S3 Event Notifications — https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/EventNotifications.html
- AWS SDK for JavaScript v3 — credential provider chain (Node) — https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sdk-for-javascript/v3/developer-guide/setting-credentials-node.html
- Block Public Access — https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/access-control-block-public-access.html
- Server-side encryption (SSE-S3 / SSE-KMS / DSSE-KMS) — https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/serv-side-encryption.html
- boto3 S3 (Python worker) — https://boto3.amazonaws.com/v1/documentation/api/latest/reference/services/s3.html
- GitHub Actions → AWS via OIDC (no static keys in CI) — https://docs.github.com/en/actions/security-for-github-actions/security-hardening-your-deployments/configuring-openid-connect-in-amazon-web-services
- Verifying Amazon SNS message signatures (if S3→SNS→consumer) — https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sns/latest/dg/sns-verify-signature-of-message.html

## Concepts (timeless)

**Auth & secrets — no long-lived keys.** Per the S3 security guide, use IAM roles supplying *temporary* credentials, not static access keys baked into the app or CI.
- GitHub Actions: authenticate via **OIDC** (`aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials` + `id-token: write`) assuming a scoped IAM role. Don't store `AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY` as a repo secret if you can avoid it.
- Both SDKs resolve credentials through a **default provider chain** (env → SSO → shared config → assumed role / web-identity → container/IMDS). Construct the client with *no* explicit credentials and let the chain resolve them — never hardcode. Don't mix sources (env var + JSON file) or you may silently run under the wrong identity.
- Next.js: keep the S3 client and any keys **server-only** (API route / server action / `import 'server-only'`). Anything in `NEXT_PUBLIC_*` ships to the browser — never put credentials there.
- The spec defers Secrets Manager for MVP (env secrets via GitHub/Vercel) — that's acceptable, but scope the IAM policy to the one bucket/prefix and least-privilege actions.

**Bucket posture (do once, verify in code/CI).**
- **Block Public Access** ON at account + bucket level. This is an artifact store; nothing here is public.
- **Encryption at rest** is on by default (SSE-S3). Use **SSE-KMS** for evidence/PII-bearing content so you get key-level access control + audit. SSE-C is now disabled by default on new buckets — don't reach for it.
- **Disable ACLs** (Object Ownership = bucket-owner-enforced); control access by bucket/IAM policy only.
- **Enforce TLS**: bucket policy `Deny` when `aws:SecureTransport=false`.
- **Versioning** ON so a re-run or bad overwrite is recoverable.

**Presigned URLs (the dashboard upload/download path).**
- A presigned URL is a **bearer token** — anyone holding it has the access. Keep TTLs short (minutes), one object key, one HTTP method (`PUT` upload / `GET` download).
- The URL inherits the permissions of the principal that signed it, and **expires when those credentials expire**, regardless of the requested TTL. SigV4 IAM-user max is 7 days; a URL signed with assumed-role/STS dies with the session (often ~1h). For a long-running LangGraph worker, this surprises people — sign close to use.
- Constrain uploads: set `ContentType`/`ContentLength` conditions (use **POST policy** / presigned POST when you need size or content-type limits the browser can't be trusted to honor). Optionally tighten with the `s3:signatureAge` bucket-policy condition.
- A `PUT` presigned upload **overwrites** an existing object with the same key — see idempotency below.

**Idempotency.** GitHub Actions jobs get retried and LangGraph nodes re-run on resume/interrupt — the same evidence/media write can happen twice.
- Make object keys **deterministic + content-addressed** (e.g. `releases/{release_run_id}/evidence/{evidence_id}` or a sha256 of the bytes), so a retry overwrites the same key rather than creating duplicates. Tie keys to your `evidence_id` / `release_run_id` contract.
- S3 `PutObject` is idempotent for identical bytes to the same key (last-writer-wins). For "create only if absent" semantics use the `If-None-Match: *` precondition header.
- Record the resulting key/version (and KMS key) in Aurora as part of provenance, not just "it was uploaded."

**Error / retry semantics.**
- The SDKs retry throttling/5xx with exponential backoff by default; tune `maxAttempts` (JS v3) / `max_attempts` + `adaptive` retry mode (botocore) rather than hand-rolling loops. Treat `503 SlowDown` as retryable, `403/404` as not.
- For large media/video assets use **multipart upload** (`@aws-sdk/lib-storage` `Upload` in TS, `upload_file`/`TransferManager` in boto3); add a lifecycle rule to **abort incomplete multipart uploads** so failed ffmpeg runs don't leak storage.
- S3 is strongly read-after-write consistent now — you can read an object immediately after a successful PUT; don't add sleeps.

**Event notifications ("webhook" verification).** If you wire S3 events (object-created → trigger), they are **at-least-once** and **not ordered** — design consumers to be idempotent and dedupe on object key + version/eTag.
- S3→**EventBridge** is the cleanest, auth-native path (no signature parsing). Prefer it.
- S3→**SNS**→HTTP endpoint: you **must verify the SNS message signature** (`SigningCertURL` from an `sns.amazonaws.com` host, confirm the cert, validate signature) and handle `SubscriptionConfirmation` — don't trust raw POST bodies. Use the AWS SNS verification libraries rather than rolling your own.
- Avoid the self-trigger loop: never let a notification handler write back into the same bucket+prefix that triggers it.

**Sandbox vs production.** Separate **buckets per environment** (not just prefixes) with separate IAM roles, so a dev credential can't touch prod evidence. Parameterize bucket name + region by env var; default the local/dev profile to a non-prod bucket. For tests, prefer **moto** (Python) / **aws-sdk-client-mock** or LocalStack (TS) over hitting real S3; never run integration tests against the prod bucket.

## Checklist

- [ ] S3 client built with **no hardcoded credentials**; relies on the provider chain / assumed role.
- [ ] CI (GitHub Actions) authenticates via **OIDC role assumption**, not static keys.
- [ ] No AWS keys in `NEXT_PUBLIC_*`, client bundles, or logs; S3 calls are server-only.
- [ ] Bucket: Block Public Access ON, ACLs disabled, versioning ON, SSE (KMS for sensitive evidence), TLS-only bucket policy.
- [ ] IAM policy scoped to the **one bucket + prefix + minimal actions** the role needs.
- [ ] Browser upload/download uses **short-TTL presigned URLs/POST**, one key + one method, signed server-side.
- [ ] Presigned-URL TTL accounts for the **signing credential's** own expiry (assumed-role sessions).
- [ ] Object keys are **deterministic/content-addressed** so retries overwrite, not duplicate; keys recorded in Aurora.
- [ ] Large assets use multipart upload + a lifecycle rule to abort incomplete uploads.
- [ ] Retries use SDK config (`maxAttempts`/retry mode), not manual loops; non-retryable errors surfaced.
- [ ] Event consumers are idempotent + dedupe; SNS signatures verified (or EventBridge used).
- [ ] Separate buckets/roles for dev vs prod; tests use moto/LocalStack, never the prod bucket.

## Gotchas

- **Presigned URL expires before its stated TTL.** Signed with STS/assumed-role creds (the norm in a GitHub Actions worker), the URL dies when the *session* ends (~1h default) even if you asked for 7 days. Symptom: `ExpiredToken`/403. Sign immediately before handing the URL out; don't pre-mint and queue.
- **`SignatureDoesNotMatch` from clock skew or proxies.** Runner/container clock drift or a corporate proxy mutating headers/query string breaks SigV4. Ensure NTP sync; reproduce without the proxy.
- **`NEXT_PUBLIC_` credential leak.** Putting an access key in a `NEXT_PUBLIC_*` var (or instantiating an S3 client in a client component) ships it to every browser. S3 calls belong in API routes / server actions only.
- **Silent overwrite on retry.** A `PUT` (incl. presigned PUT) to an existing key replaces it with no warning. With non-deterministic keys you get duplicate objects on retry; with the same key you can clobber good data. Use content-addressed keys + `If-None-Match: *` for create-only.
- **At-least-once + unordered events.** S3 notifications can arrive twice and out of order, and can take >1 minute. A consumer that assumes exactly-once or chronological order will double-process. Dedupe on key+eTag/versionId.
- **Unverified SNS/HTTP webhook.** Treating a raw S3→SNS→HTTP POST as trusted lets anyone forge events. Verify the SNS signature (and `SigningCertURL` host) or use EventBridge.
- **Notification self-trigger loop.** A handler that writes to the same bucket+prefix that triggered it re-invokes itself — runaway cost. Use a separate output bucket/prefix.
- **Leaked incomplete multipart uploads.** Failed ffmpeg/video assembly leaves partial multipart uploads that you pay for and that never appear as objects. Add a lifecycle rule to abort them after N days.
- **SSE-C reflex.** SSE-C is now disabled by default on new buckets and can't be read by AWS services. For this artifact store use SSE-S3 or SSE-KMS — don't enable SSE-C unless a workload truly requires it.
- **Bucket-name namespace squatting.** Don't delete-and-recreate buckets between runs; a deleted global bucket name can be claimed by another account. Empty and keep buckets instead.
- **Mixed credential sources.** Relying on both an env var and a loaded JSON/profile means a config change can silently switch the identity your writes run under — pin one source per environment.
